ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Shows (a) synthesis procedure and growth mechanism of cds nanorods and nanoflowers with varying solvothermal time. SEM images of (b) bare CdS NRs, (c) CQD@CdS12NFs, (d) CQD@CdS24NFs. TEM images and d-spacing for (e, f) CQD@CdS12NFs and (g, h) CQD@CdS24NFs. Elemental mapping of (i-l) CQD@CdS12NFs.
Shows diffraction pattern (a), raman spectra (b), ftir spectra (c), surface area for all cds samples.
Shows xps full survey scan (a), cd 3d spectrum (b), C 1s spectrum (c) and S 2p spectrum (d) for all CdS samples.
Shows absorbance spectra (a), photoluminescence emission spectra (b), energy bandgap tau-plot (c) and mott-schottky plot (d) for all cds samples.
Shows the transient responsive photoluminescence decay spectra (a), electro impedance spectra (b), electron paramagnetic spectra (c) and transient photocurrent for all cds samples.

Shows rhodamine b absorbance decay profile for (a) bare cds nrs, (b) cqd@cds12nfs, (c) cqd@cds24nfs. (d) concentration decay profile as a function of time, (e) linear fit obtained for pseudo-second order kinetic model (f) real-time color change of dye getting degraded (g) seed germination of Vigna Radiata for all CdS samples. The different parameters for plant growth (h) germination percentage and (i) average length of shoots and roots.

Shows (a) time dependent photocatalytic h2 evolution, (b) stability test for H2 evolution, (c) H2 evolution rate in absence of sacrificial reagents and its respective AQY, (d) H2 evolution rate in presence of sacrificial reagents with respective to their Svacancy for all CdS samples. (e) comparative literature survey for CdS based H2 evolution.
B shows (a) band alignment for different cds morphology and (b) possible mechanism for photocatalytic hydrogen generation.
Intensity ratios for the planes (200) and (100), crystallite size, dislocation density and average strain for all CdS samples.
Energy bandgap, flat band potential, conduction band and valance band for all CdS samples.
Shows kinetic analysis of emission decay for the cds samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the previous decade, the global primary energy consumption has increased by an average of 1.7 %, which has attained 17.9 TW in 2017 [1]. Projections further estimate this into 22 TW by 2030, an almost 25 % increase, with fossil fuels accounting for nearly 80 % of the total [2,3]. The undeniable reliance of the world on fossil fuels has raised concerns from a sustainability and environmental standpoint. While thermo and electrocatalysts in the chemical industry have addressed some issues by reducing energy input, achieving bias-free photocatalytic overall water splitting (OWS) into hydrogen (H2) and oxygen (O2) remains a long-term goal for sustainable fuel production [4–8].

Photocatalytic OWS, unlike its highly studied photo-electro-chemical counterpart, eliminates the need for conductive electrolytes or strong acidic/alkaline solutions. This reduces utilization costs, which in turn addresses multiple stability and safety concerns [9]. Nevertheless, challenges exists which are directly related to its narrow visible-light-response range and undesirable charge carrier recombination, resulting in a limited apparent quantum yield (AQY) efficiency (typically below 15 %) that is reported from literature [10]. The reported content of UV light in the 300–400 nm region in the solar spectrum is less than 3 % [11]. Despite almost having 40 % of solar light falling within the visible spectrum found at 400–700 nm [12], current visible-light-responsive (VLR) catalysts are typically limited to 400–485 nm, hindering energy conversion efficiency [13]. To maximize the efficiency of photocatalytic water splitting systems, the selection of an appropriate photocatalyst that can efficiently absorb longer wavelengths of solar spectrum is very crucial.

Among the various semiconductors, ZnO, TiO2 and Bi2Te3 possess excellent catalytic activity and stability, but severely limited to UV or IR region [14,15,77]. Alternatively, considerable efforts have been focused on developing photocatalysts using metal sulfides such as cadmium sulphide (CdS) have emerged as one of the most promising materials for OWS, due to its narrow energy bandgap (2.4 eV) and favorable band potential positions [16,17]. Nevertheless, bare CdS still cannot meet the requirement in practical application because of the high photo corrosion and charge recombination rate.

It has been reported that VLR-hydrogen evolution activity of CdS is highly dependent on its morphology [18], crystal structure [19], particle size [20]. These factors can directly control the quantity of photons impacting the catalyst surface through the manipulation of its effective surface area and adjustment of its band gap structure. Up to now, numerous methods have been explored to fabricate CdS nanocrystals with different morphologies, such as nano leaves [21], nanosheets [18] and nanorods, multi-armed nanopods, [22] and microtowers. Depending on morphology, these catalysts can be classified on the basis of electron confinement: 0D (quantum dots) have confined electrons in all directions. In contrast, one-dimensional (1D) catalysts, such as nanorods, confine electrons in one direction, while two-dimensional (2D) catalysts, like sheets, confine electrons in two directions. Therefore, based on confinement different dimensions could be ordered as 0D > 1D > 2D > 3D. With increased confinement, the larger driving force will be required to excite charge carriers and thereby leading to increased bandgap. Thus, to obtain superior catalytic performance, bandgap can be tuned through varying the morphology of photocatalyst to avail higher solar light absorption.

CdS morphology can be manipulated through various synthesis methods, such as ultrasonic microemulsion [20], chemical bath deposition [21], chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [22,23], hydrothermal [24], and solvothermal methods [25]. Among these, the hydrothermal method stands out as one of the most commonly employed for CdS nanocrystal synthesis due to its simplicity. By adjusting parameters like temperature, time, precursors, and solvent, it becomes feasible to attain well-controlled size and shape in the CdS nanocrystals.

The morphology CdS can be controlled through a variety of methods, including ultrasonic microemulsion [23], chemical bath deposition [24], chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [25,26], hydrothermal [27] and solvothermal method [28]. Among these, the hydrothermal method stands out as one of the most commonly employed for CdS nanocrystals due to its facile nature. Through careful control of the adjusting the parameters such as temperature, time, precursors, and solvent it becomes feasible to attain well-controlled shape and size of CdS nanocrystals. Wang et al. [18] reported superior photocatalytic activity of ZnO nanoflowers due to presence of more defect states compared nanorods. In a similar study, Guo et al. [19] explored the effect of CdS active facets by varying the injection rate of S2− ions, which exhibited (002) facet promising photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 1.61 mmolg−1h−1 with an excellent stability. Furthermore, shenoy et al. [29] reported synthesis of 3D nanorod clusters as photocatalyst for erioglaucine degradation. The catalyst demonstrated impressive efficiency, by degrading erioglaucine up to 97 % in just 50 min.

These studies have demonstrated morphology has a significant role in enhancement of photocatalytic activity by changing its crystal facets and by creating point defects (SRH recombination centers) that helps in increasing charge carrier’s lifetime. Depending on the position of these defect states, it can be allocated to either shallow defect and deep defects. Shallow defects, also known as point defects, have the capability to adjust Fermi levels, augment carrier concentration and enhance the conductivity of the photocatalyst. In contrast, deep defects hinder carrier transport and reduce carrier diffusion length, resulting in significant loss of VOC and consequently limiting catalyst improvement. Thus, careful introduction of shallow defects and the mitigation of detrimental deep defects becomes significant to enhance the hydrogen evolution activity. Additionally, the morphology of nanocrystals also plays a substantial role on crystal facets. These exposed facets can influence the adsorption/activation ability of active sites, thereby enhancing the surface reactivity of the photocatalyst. For instance, Liu et al. reported improved H2 yield 5.98 mmolg−1h−1 of CdS with exposed facets (002) having highest surface energy. Similarly, Dang et al. reported H2 evolution of 3100 µmol g−1 h−1 with CdS nanospheres having dominant (002) facet that provided larger surface area. Although these studies provided an effective way for solar water splitting, the quest for substantial hydrogen generation using a solitary photocatalyst has remained an ongoing challenge. Furthermore, a conspicuous gap in the existing literatures becomes evident when considering the scarcity of comprehensive investigations into CdS (002) exposed face and dearth of reports addressing implications of morphology-induced defects and its effect on charge carrier dynamics.

To overcome this limitation, formation of cocatalyst heterostructure has emerged as a promising approach in enhancing efficiency of OWA systems. Among the various used materials, non-noble metal-based co-catalysts such as Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) have attained high attention of researchers. These CQDs are excellent co-catalyst due to its exceptional properties as excellent electron acceptors, non-toxicity, low cost, high stability and providing larger surface area [30–32] making them highly favorable compare to its other allotrope graphene. Zhu et al. [33] reported H2 evolution of 170 µmol g−1 h−1 using CdS as parent photocatalyst with an excellent stability in harsh seawater conditions due to CQDs loading. In another study, Gogoi et al. [22] introduced a novel approach by combining two heterojunctions, CQD/CdS-CdO nanowires, resulting in a significant increase in H2 generation rate to 309 mmol g−1 h−1. The formation of CdS-CdO heterostructure greatly enhanced the stability of CdS, protecting it from photocorrosion. Similarly, Smrithi et al. [34] developed CQD/CdS nanospheres that demonstrated a remarkable 97 % degradation of Rhodamine B within 120 min, and under visible light, they achieved an H2 generation rate of 1600 µmol g−1 h−1. While all these studies show progress, still the amount of hydrogen generated is significantly low and further improvements are necessary to make it viable for real-time applications.

Herein we report for first time the effect of morphology on shallow defects creation in photocatalyst. It was found that a variation in morphology or size of CdS, significantly affect the defects states and crystal facets. Further, a synergistic effect of CQD loading on these CdS nanostructures was studied for Hydrogen Evolution and Rhodamine B dye degradation under visible light conditions. It was found the presence of (002) face oriented CdS provided an improved stability and presence of sulphur vacancy (Svacancy ) reduced the recombination of charge carriers and thereby also increased catalytic action.

All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purifications. Cadmium acetate (Cd(CH3COO)2), thiourea ((NH2)2CS), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), glucose (C6H12O6), urea (CO(NH2)2) and ethylenediamine (C2H4(NH2)2) and was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (USA). DI water (resistivity: 18.5 MΩ-cm) was used for all analysis.
The detailed steps for carbon quantum dots synthesis have been provided in the supplemental information section S1.2.

CdS nanorods were prepared by solvothermal process according to previously reported literature with additional modifications. Typically, 1.66 g of cadmium acetate (Cd(CH3COO)2) and 0.49 g of thiourea ((NH2)2CS) was dissolved in 25 mL C2H4(NH2)2 and stirred for 1 h under 70 °C. Followed by the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave, and reaction was carried out under 180 °C for 24 h. Once it has cooled down to room temperature, the yellow color obtained product was separated via centrifugation (2500 rpm and 15 min) and washed with ethanol for several times. The residue was placed under heating conditions at 60 °C to get dry and finally bare CdS NRs was obtained.

The synthesis process of CQD@CdS nanoflowers were similar to CdS nanorods. 1.66 g of cadmium acetate (Cd(CH3COO)2) and 0.49 g of thiourea ((NH2)2CS) was dissolved in 9.5 mL and 12.5 mL of C2H4(NH2)2 respectively. Further, water soluble CQD (3 mL) added to aqueous cadmium salt solution and resultant was stir for 30 min. Dropwise thiourea solution was added to the above mixture and again it was stirred for 30 min under 70 °C. Followed by the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave, and reaction was carried out under two different conditions 180 °C for 12 h and 24 h. Once it gets cooling down to room temperature, the yellow color obtained product separated via centrifugation (2500 rpm and 15 min) and washed with ethanol several times. The residue was placed at oven at 60 °C to get dry and finally CQD@CdS12NFs and CQD@CdS24NFs was obtained.
The detailed experimental details of photocatalytic hydrogen generation and Rhodamine B degradation have been provided in supporting file S1.3 and S1.4 respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth mechanism: Facet dependent CdS nanoflowers and nanorods formation
ce:section.ce:para:
The selection of solvent in solvothermal process plays a critical role in defining structure and morphology of nano crystals. For instance, O containing solvent, TEA can introduce impurity into CdS lattice, which can destroy its intrinsic crystal structure [18]. Here, we are using O free EDA as solvent which contained N atoms that coordinates with Cd of exposed (100) and (010) facets. This slows down the growth speed in (100) and (010) direction and continues its subsequent growth along (002) plane. Besides that the small molecular chain of EDA favors its vertical adsorption on CdS surface with slightly weaker bond [18,35]. Due to this, although CdS nanorods were successfully formed but aggregated to constitute nanorod cluster assemblies [36]. The involved chemical reactions as shown in following Eqn. (1–4):
(1) Cd2+ + 2(EDA) → [Cd(EDA)2]2+
(2) (NH2)2CS + H2O → 2NH2 + H2S + CO2
(3) Cd(EDA) x
2+ + S2− → CdS(EDA) x
(4)
C d S
E D A
x
→ Δ
C d S
x - y

( E D A )

However, when water contained CQD solution were introduced to the reaction mixture, the viscosity and solubility of the system changed. As EDA solution is an alkaline in nature, N atoms attract H+ and consequently N-Cd bond strength weeks due to resulting balance between N–Cd and N–H+ bonding. This helped in restricting growth along (100) and (010) planes and promoted faster growth rate along (002) plane [19]. Further width of nanorods increases and tends to become nanosheets that aggregate and self-assemble into hierarchical flowerlike structure to minimization of surface energy. The detailed synthesis process has been shown in Fig. 1
a.

The influence of varying time and addition of CQD in the CdS samples was studied by SEM and TEM images. Fig. 1 b shows the SEM images of Bare CdS NRs. The presence of pure EDA solvent gave rise to formation of CdS nanorods that agglomerated to form nanorod clusters. These developed nanoclusters are 5 µm. However, when CQD solution were introduced to reaction mixture, CdS morphology changes from nanorods to petalus nanoflowers (Fig. 1 c and Fig. 1 e). Further, these nanoflowers changed size with varying reaction time. The nanoflowers synthesized for 12 h (CQD@CdS12NFs) have an order of 3.30 µm, whereas for 24 h (CQD@CdS24NFs) have 2.85 µm. Similar observation was observed in other reported literatures, where this decrease in size can be due to breakdown larger nanoflowers into smaller ones as time proceeds (Fig. 1 d and Fig. 1 g) [37–40]. These petals became compact with increasing time forming nanoflowers. The TEM images Fig. 1 f and Fig. 1 h show d- spacing values of 3.35 Å and 3.34 Å for the samples CQD@CdS12NFs and CQD@CdS24NFs confirms their hexagonal phase formation.

Further, no significant change in morphology was observed due to CQD on CdS surface. However, their presence and smaller size of nanoflowers led to an increase in their surface area as shown in Fig. 2
d. The presence of CQD on CdS nanostructures can be seen in Fig. S3
a, where the small black dots represent CQDs. The synthesized CQDs were found to be ∼3 nm ( Fig. S3
b). The elemental mapping shown in Fig. 1 i-l and Fig. S4
a-e confirms the presence of elemental Cd, S, C and O in CQD@CdS12NFs and CQD@CdS24NFs respectively. Additionally, the point EDAX spectrum in Fig. S4
f-h of CQD@CdS24NFs confirms the highest decrease in atomic% of sulphur. Table S1 shows the decreased intensity ratio of Cd/S that resembles with XPS and EPR analysis [41]. This shows the CdS with higher reaction time had more sulphur vacancies, that acts as trap centers for photoinduced electrons thereby reducing the recombination rate and increasing their stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and elemental analysis of photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized nanostructures often have surface defects, which are caused by dislocations, vacancies, and strain in lattice planes. These defects can act as traps for photo induced charge carriers and adsorption sites. When electrons transfer to these adsorbed sites, it can prevent the recombination of photo-excited electron-hole pairs [42]. Recent studies have shown that increasing the number of surface defects can enhance charge separation and improve catalytic activity [43]. Here from Table 1 , we can observe CQD@CdS24NFs and bare CdS NRs has high average strain and dislocation density, this can ascribe due to presence of sulphur vacancies confirmed through XPS and EDAX analysis. Thus, helping for better H2 evolution and better dye degradation.

The study presents, synthesis of CQD loaded hexagonal wurtzite phase CdS nanoflowers and nanorods. Observations indicate that the hexagonal phase of CdS demonstrates superior photocatalytic performance when compared to other phases, and this can be attributed to several factors. Firstly, the hexagonal phase of CdS boasts an enlarged surface area [44], promoting enhanced interaction [45] with light and other molecules, thereby augmenting its photocatalytic activity. Secondly, the hexagonal phase of CdS exhibits higher electron mobility, facilitating the efficient transfer of electrons during the catalytic process [46]. Consequently, this leads to accelerated reaction rates and improved overall efficiency. Further, it is clearly demonstrated from XRD pattern, bare CdS NRs shows a preferential growth in (100), (002) and (101) planes. But for CQD@CdS NFs the preferential growth found to be in (002) plane and restricted its growth in the direction (100) and (101) planes that helps in formation of nanoflowers (as discussed in above sections). The diffraction intensity ratios of CdS samples for the planes I(200)/(101) and I(200)/(100) has been mentioned in Table 1. Liu et al., demonstrated the facet dependent H2 evolution activity by CdS samples, that showed CdS nanostructures highly oriented towards (002) plane are stable and effectively take part in water splitting action [19]. This supports our results, CQD@CdSNFs having higher photocatalytic activity and superior stability compared to bare CdS NRs.

Raman spectroscopy has been used to determine the vibrational and structural properties of bare CdS NRs and CQD@CdS NFs (Fig. 2 b). The raman signals for all CdS samples show two major characteristics peaks at around 300 cm−1 and 600 cm−1 referred to fundamental longitudinal optical phonon mode (1LO) and its overtone (2LO) mode respectively. The 1LO and 2LO mode for bare CdS NRs were observed at 289.99 cm−1 and 587.14 cm−1. However, with the addition of CQD both CQD@CdS12NFs and CQD@CdS24NFs showed a peak shift towards higher wavenumber 292.07 cm−1 and 589.22 cm−1 for 1LO and 2LO mode respectively. This confirms the presence of CQD on CdS surface. Two additional peaks at 1300 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 were observed in CQD@CdSNFs corresponding to D and G bands of CQD. The occurrence of 1LO and 2LO mode at same peak for CQD@CdS12NFs and CQD@CdS24NFs reveals, the raman spectrum is not dependent on size of particle. However, after CQD loading Raman signals significantly increased compared to bare CdS NRs, this can be explained in terms of exciton-phonon coupling (EPC) and surface defects. Raman spectroscopy is greatly governed by EPC, that highly dependents on dimension of nanoparticle [47]. The 1D nanorods shows strong confinement effect and have different modes of polarization, that can lead to modification of phonon dispersion and act as a result the energies of both 1LO and 2LO looks identical [48]. Further, in case of CdS NFs, increase in carrier concentration by Svacancy also leads to enhancement of raman signals.

The IR spectra of CdS samples confirms the formation of CdS and different functional group it contains as shown in Fig. 2 c. The characteristic peak features for all CdS samples centered at 3353 cm−1 and 1637 cm−1 corresponds to stretching modes of hydroxyl group (–OH) and H–O–H bending vibrations of water molecules, respectively. The peak at 632 cm−1 attributed to Cd-S bonds, that confirms the formation of CdS nanostructures. The presence of CQD in CQD@CdS12NFs and CQD@CdS24NFs creates an additional band at 2926 cm−1 that states CQD is attached on CdS surface through –NH2 bonding. The peak at 1550–1650 cm−1 corresponds to −C=O/C=C stretching of aromatic hydrocarbons [33]. The range of peaks near to 1070 cm−1 and 636 cm−1 ascribed to −C–O–C stretching vibrations of ether and C–OH stretching modes of alcohol, emphasizing the hydrophilic behavior of CQD [49].

The FTIR spectra of CQD has been shown in Fig S2 . CQDs are formed by breakdown and condensation of large glucose molecules, that shows a bending vibration of –CH2 at 765 cm−1. The peak at 1593 cm−1, 2882 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1 correspond to C=C bond and stretching vibrations of C–H [50]. The presence of –OH groups were confirmed from the peak at 3350 cm−1. This confirms the synthesized CQDs are water soluble.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BET surface and pore size distribution
ce:section.ce:para: The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and the associated pore size distribution profiles for as-prepared CdS samples are shown in Fig. 2 d and Fig S5 . All the samples can be classified as mesoporous materials based on their form, [51], which can be assigned to Type-IV. The average pore size, pore volume and surface area for all samples are listed in Table S3 . It can be seen that the adsorption–desorption isotherms show significant difference with varying the morphology of CdS nanostructures [52]. As we can observe, surface area for bare CdS NRs showed lowest, it got increased as CQD was loaded on the CdS NFs. Also, CdS nanoflowers have 3D structure composed, results in a higher surface area because of its larger exposed surfaces. While nanorods have offered high surface area in many cases. However, here the clustering of nanorods reduced its overall accessible surfaces that contributing to lower surface area compared to nanoflowers. Further, due to smaller size of CQD@CdS24NFs, its surface area found to significantly higher compared to CQD@CdS12NFs.

To determine the surface chemical composition and valance states of the developed photocatalysts, the XPS analysis was performed. The total survey scan of the elements confirms the presence of Cd, S, C (Fig. 3
a). In Fig. 3 b, the resolution XPS spectra of Cd 3d shows two doublets at 404.4 eV and 411.1 eV, which can assign to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 respectively for all the samples [53]. Additionally, there was no change in peak position, which confirms the absence of any bond formation due to the addition of CQD. The XPS peak for S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 was observed at binding energies bare CdS NRs (160.8 eV, 162.1 eV), CQD@CdS12NFs (160.8 eV, 161.9 eV) and CQD@CdS24NFs (160.8 eV, 161.9 eV). As shown in Fig. 3 d, S2p3/2 peak does not show any peak shift with the presence of CQD. However, S2p1/2 shifted towards lower binding energy side in sample CQD@CdS12NFs and CQD@CdS24NFs. This change in peak position of S2p1/2 can be ascribed due to its bond formation with CQD. Also, in FTIR spectra, CQD loaded samples had an additional peak at 2926 cm−1 confirming its bonding with S atoms. This shows the CQDs are strongly coupled on the surface of CdS nanoflowers, which increases their stability (discussed in later sections). The deconvoluted C 1s spectra of CQD@CdS12NFs (284.4 eV, 285.1 eV, 286.4 eV and 288 eV) and CQD@CdS24NFs (284.3 eV, 285 eV, 286.2 eV and 287.8 eV) has been illustrated in Fig. 3 c, assigned to C=C of sp2 carbon, C–C/CH of sp3 carbon, C–O/C=O and O–C=O/COOH. The lower peak shift of C 1s spectra tells about increase in electron cloud density of CdS, due to strong chemical interaction between CQD and CdS [41,54].

Further, the S2p1/2 peak shift of towards lower binding energy with deceased peak intensity shows its electronegative nature and their absence causing Svacancy
[55,56]. The Table S2 shows the atomic % of S, Cd, C and O elements present in the samples. The increase in Svacancy in CQD@CdS24NFs, is directly proportional with reaction timing [57]. The CQD@CdS12NFs had lowest Svacancy with 28 % which was also confirmed by its small peak in EPR measurements. Further, the nature of O 1s was studied and has been shown in Fig S11. A peak shift towards lower binding energy can be identified in CdS nanoflowers that justifies the doping of elemental oxygen by creating sulphur vacancies [78]. The similar behavior has been observed in other metal sulfides such as In2S3 [58]and MoS2 [59].

The absorbance spectrum was recorded to investigate the optical properties of bare CdS NRs and CQD@CdS NFs has been shown in Fig. 4
a. A strong absorption onset was exhibited by all CdS samples in visible range of 490–505 nm. Interestingly, a blue shift was seen in the order bare CdS NRs < CQD@CdS12NFs < CQD@CdS24NFs, that was governed by the size of CdS nanostructures confirmed from SEM analysis. Similar observations were reported by Jin et al., that demonstrated morphological variation in ZnCdS nanostructures causes structural defects and strain can lead to broadening of energy bandgap [60]. Based on above results, the corresponding bandgap can be calculated using Eqn. 5.
(5) (αhν) = A(hν − Eg)1/2

Whereas A, h, α, ν and Eg proportionality constant, planks constant, absorption coefficient, frequency of incident light and bandgap respectively [61]. The bandgap was calculated from a plot of (αhν)2 vs the photon energy (Eg) as presented in Fig. 4 c. The bandgap of bare CdS NRs, CQD@CdS12NFs and CQD@CdS24NFs was found to be 2.27 eV, 2.33 eV and 2.38 eV respectively. The larger Eg of CQD@CdS24NFs can be attributed to its smaller size of and presence of Svacancy that found to match well with the previous reports. In addition to width of Eg, the CB edge positions of semiconductor also plays an important factor in enhancement of photocatalytic activity. It is known that semiconductors with a more negative CB edge possesses stronger reducing power for the H2 production [62]. The CB positions of respective samples have been shown in Fig. 8 b. It can be clearly seen the CB edge of CQD@CdS24NFs found to be more negative band potential compared to other samples. However, the large Eg of CQD@CdS24NFs is not conducive to efficient photocatalysis. Typically, achieving optimal H2 generation involves a suitable with appropriate CB positions. Therefore, CQD@CdS12NFs attains higher efficiency in VLR photocatalysis by striking a balance between light absorption capacity and reduction power [63]. The summarized results have been shown in Table 2 .

Further, the absorption spectra of CQD have been shown in Fig. S6 . It reveals two absorption band peaks in the 200–300 nm range. The peak at 230 nm is associated with π–π* transition of the aromatic C=C bond with sp2 hybridization in regions while the peak at 300 nm is attributed to n–π* transition of the carbonyl or amine groups present on the surface.

To elucidate the governing the effect of CQD and morphology on photocatalytic hydrogen generation of CdS, the energy level diagram was studied. The Mott-Schottky (M−S) plots of all the CdS samples were determined in a 0.2 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution using the Eqn. 6. The flat band potential was determined by calculating the point of intersection between the tangent line of the M−S curves and the x-axis from these graphs. According to Fig. 4 d flat band potentials of samples were estimated to be −0.0338, −0.2946, −0.5232 vs SCE for bare CdS NRs, CQD@CdS12NFs and CQD@CdS24NFs respectively. As CB of N-type semiconductor more negative than its flat band potential [64], thus the final CB potential was converted to potential corresponding to NHE (ENHE) using Eqn. 7 as shown in Table 2. The obtained ECB values for bare CdS NRs, CQD@CdS12NFs and CQD@CdS24NFS was 0.2062, −0.0546, −0.2832 vs NHE calculated from Eqn. 8 [65].
(6) 1/C2 = 2 (NDeεε0 )−1 .(E − EFB − kT/e)

Where C is capacitance of the space charge region, ND is charge carrier concentration, e is electron charge, ε is dielectric constant, ε0 is the vacuum permittivity, E is electrode applied potential, EFB is flat band potential, k is Boltzmann constant and T is absolute temperature.
(7) ENHE=ESCE+0.24 V
(8) ECB=EVB − Eg

The separation efficiency of charge carriers of CdS nanorods and nanoflowers was studied by photoluminescence spectra as shown in Fig. 4 b. A decreased intensity in the PL spectrum suggests efficient separation of photogenerated excitons, whereas elevated intensity is linked to their rapid recombination [66,67]. The PL spectra of all CdS samples were excited at 380 nm and intrinsic emission band was found to be at 437 nm. The high intensity for bare CdS NRs stems from the higher recombination rate of photoinduced charge carriers. Also, CQD@CdS12NFs showed lowest PL intensity with an additional peak at 526 nm, suggesting less charge carrier recombination and longer lifetime (Fig. 5
a) due to presence of extra surface states in its CB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and surface defects analysis of photocatalyst

ce:section.ce:para:
To further evaluate the efficacy of photo-generated charge carriers of the as-prepared photocatalysts, time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay behaviors was examined. Photocatalysts, when excited by photons of suitable wavelength, prompt electrons to move to a higher energy level, initiating redox reactions. Conversely, when electrons return to the ground state, they release energy through fluorescence radiation, indicating the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes. Consequently, the PL intensity serves as an indicator of photocatalytic activity. The decay curves for all CdS samples have been shown in Fig. 5 a and other parameters such as positive decay components time and percentage has been presented in Table 3 . The decreased intensity of CQD@CdS24NFs shows it has better charge separation efficiency that is a greater number of charge carriers can participate in reaction. Also, the average lifetime (τavg) of all samples was calculated using Eqn. (9) and for bare CdS NRs, CQD@CdS12NFs was found to be 7.92 ns, 12.174 ns respectively and CQD@CdS24NFs had longest lifetime with 12.886 ns. This supports its long-term stability, which is beneficial for photocatalytic H2 generation.
(9)

∑
i =

, 3

, 3

The EIS spectra of bare CdS NRs and CQD@CdSNFs under light irradiation has been shown in Fig. 5 b. The arc radius in EIS plot represents the charge separation and transport resistance between water and photoelectrode. The arc radius of CQD@CdS12NFs and CQD@CdS24NFs reduced compared to bare CdS NRs that confirms their better faster charge transport kinetics, and which can be also seen in Photocurrent and PL observations. The data were fitted to an equivalent circuit model ( Fig S7 ) and reported fitting parameters in Table S4 . The CQD@CdS12NFs shows lowest resistance (202.9 Ω) among all the developed photocatalysts that supports its superior hydrogen generation activity. As we know, resistance and conductivity are inversely proportional to each other, the lower resistance of CQD@CdS12NFs signifies the increase in electrical conductivity (9.5305 × 10−7) which was due to electron acceptor CQD on its surface. In a study, Mahanta et al. [68] demonstrated the superior charge storage ability of Boron Nitride quantum dots with an increased capacitance value that helps electron-hole separation in WO3 based photocatalyst. Similarly, the increased capacitance value of CQD@CdS12NFs was due to presence of CQD that acts as buffer to store conduction bands electrons of CdS photocatalyst.

To assess the efficiency of charge separation in the materials, transient photocurrent analysis was performed. The photocurrent of the bare CdS NRs, CQD@12CdSNFs and CQD@CdS24NFs was observed in chronoamperometry study (i-t) curves for light-on and light-off conditions shown in Fig. 5 d. The average photocurrent density of the CQD@CdS12NFs and CQD@CdS24NFs was 70 µA/cm2 and 43 µA/cm2 at potential vs NHE, which was nearly 7 times and 4.5 times higher than bare CdS NRs. This shows the morphology of the semiconductor places a key role in defining charge separation ability of photoelectrode. As discussed in earlier sections the 3D structure of nanoflowers helps it to reduce recombination rate and thus more charge carriers are able participate in reaction up to a longer time [69,70]. Further, an interesting observation was found as we reduce the size of nanoflowers they attained higher stability that can be attributed to higher growth orientation in (002) plane. This can be confirmed from the higher intensity ratios of CQD@CdS24NFs I(200)/(101) and I(200)/(100) compared to CQD@CdS12NFs (Table 1). Further, as the size of the CdS nanoflowers reduced, the confinement of electrons increases, shows a higher resistance ( Table S4 ) to participate in reactions. Whereas larger structures have more tendency to participate in reaction (due to lower resistance), as a result they easily get dissociated and not able to sustain for long time.

All CdS samples show resonance signals at g = 0.19. The weak signal for bare CdS NRs and prepared by hydrothermal method involve low Svacancy . However, when morphology was changed to nanoflowers the ESR response also changed significantly. For nanoflowers synthesized at less hydrothermal timing (CQD@CdS12NFs) showed had less sulphur vacancies compared to those synthesized at higher hydrothermal timing (CQD@CdS24NFs). These results also resemble with XPS elemental results shown in Table S2 . Further, the presence of Svacancy acts as trap centers where, electrons get trapped in these defects states that reduces the probability of electron-hole recombination. He et al., reported the presence of Svacancy also raises the valance band of photocatalyst which can be also observed in our synthesized samples (Fig. 5 c) [57]. Furthermore, Svacancy can boost the presence of free charge carriers, enhance the mobility of electrolyte ions, and reveal additional active sites, which all have a positive contribution to electrochemical activity.

The assessment of photocatalytic efficiency for the developed nanoflowers was conducted through comparative analysis with bare CdS NRs, employing RhB dye as the model target molecule. To assess the degradation efficiency of photocatalysts, 25 mg of each catalyst was used for 50 mg/L concentration of RhB dye and lowering of absorption peak (Fig. 6
a-c) was monitored under visible light condition. The Fig. 6 d shows the degradation efficiency achieved by bare CdS NRs was 94.7 %, CQD@CdS12NFs was 95.90 % and CQD@Cds24NFs was 94.82 % in 210 min, 120 min respectively. bare CdS NRs reached its saturation point after 180 min, this can be due to strong adsorption of dye components on catalyst surface making the catalyst completely oxidized. The real time change in color after 30 min and final degraded dye for all sample has been shown in Fig. 6 f. To understand the kinetics and mechanism of RhB dye mineralization with optimized catalytic loading, Langmuir-Hinshelwood and pseudo-second order kinetic models were utilized using as Eqn. 10,
(10) ln (C/C0) = −Kappt

C0 = initial concentration and Ct = final concentration. The pseudo-second order kinetics was calculated with Eqn. 11. A linear fit between ln (1/C) against t shown in Fig. 6 e.
(11) (1/ C) − (1/C0) = Kappt

Further, a comparative analysis on degrading RhB dye using other reported materials has been discussed in Table S5 . Natarajan et al. [71] reported R6G degradation of 96 % in 180 min using Degussa P-25 TiO2 nano catalysts. Ullah et al. [72] showed synthesis of CdS nanorods and catalytic R6G degradation up to 88.4 %. Similarly, CdS nanowires were also used for RhB degradation which showed an efficiency of 97 % in 180 min. A unique Y-shaped CQD embedded on CdS nanorods was reported by Moniruzzaman et al. [73] in which they successfully degraded 98 % of R6G dye in 45 min. Herein we report synthesis of CQD loaded morphology varying CdS nanostructures by solvothermal process. The superior degradation efficiency by CQD@CdS12NFs can be due to presence of CQD with more Svacancy that helps reducing recombination rate.

Further, a shift of absorbance was observed as dye concentration gradually decreases. [74] This can be due to CQD@CdS12NFs follows breakdown of RhB chromophores degradation pathway whereas bare CdS NRs and CQD@CdS24NFs favors photodegradation of RhB with N-deethylation pathway. This is evident from the main peak shifting from 557 nm to 503 nm and 554 nm to 547 nm for bare CdS NRs and CQD@CdS24NFs respectively.

Phytotoxicity experiments were conducted to assess the suitability of pre-treated water for irrigation. Fig. 6 g illustrates the phytotoxicity of untreated and treated RhB and DY solutions through a seed germination assay. The resilient nature of RhB is evident as the untreated solution significantly hampers the seed germination Vigna Radiata when compared to treated and DI solutions in 72 h. The presence of RhB molecules in water influences both plant length and seed germination rate. The seed germination percentage values for control (DI water) were 100 %, RhB solution shows 70 % and degraded RhB solution shows 82.2 % (Fig. 6 h). Another length profiling study was also conducted (Fig. 6 i) showed that degraded dye solution is fewer toxic favors the growth of plants. These findings suggest that photocatalytic water treatment can provide an alternative for excessive usage of underground water. These results suggest that photocatalytic water treatment offers an alternative to excessive use of underground water and encourages the use of treated water for irrigating golf courses, parks, and gardens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalyst testing for solar-water-splitting hydrogen generation
ce:section.ce:para:
Hydrogen evolution activity of the developed photocatalysts were tested under visible light conditions. The photocatalyst was suspended in 12 ml of DI water and hydrogen evolution was measured using gas chromatography. No appreciable hydrogen was produced (Fig. 7
d) in absence of sacrificial reagents. This was due to lower lifetime of photogenerated charge carriers that causes recombination, ultimately giving less hydrogen generation rate. To overcome this, here we use Methanol and Triethylamine (TEA) in 1:1 ratio added along with photocatalyst into the suspension. TEA acts sacrificial electron donor and methanol is used to reduce phase segregation between TEA and water. The time dependent H2 evolution activity for over period of 10hr for all samples has been shown in Fig. 7 a. The cumulative hydrogen generation after 10hr for bare CdS NRs, CQD@CdS12NFs and CQD@CdS24NFs was found 58,195 µmol g−1, 96,389 µmol g−1, 120,748 µmol g−1 respectively. This shows presence of CQD significantly affects the hydrogen generation rate (Fig. 7 c). CQD as good source of electron acceptor, acts as an electronic buffer for CB electrons of CdS. The generated electrons get trapped in Svacancy further moving towards CQD. This helps in separating the electron-hole pair recombination thereby increasing the H2 yield. Further, Fig S8 shows the H2 generation rate with different concentration (15, 25, 50 mg) of photocatalyst. The rate of hydrogen generation increased with the loading of the photocatalyst up to 25 mg, after which it started to decrease. The higher concentrations led to turbidity in the aqueous suspension, thereby reducing light intensity.

Further, reusability of photocatalyst was studied with 3 consecutive cycles up to 30 h (Fig. 7 b). After every 10 h, the photocatalyst was washed and the glass chamber was purged with Ar gas. It was observed that H2 evolution remained almost constant for the first two cycles but reduced in the third cycle, possibly due to the oxidation of the radical scavenger during the redox reaction. From the graph it can be interpreted that, H2 rate of CQD@CdS12NFs reduced and reached up to level of CQD@CdS24NFs. Moreover, SEM analysis of photocatalyst was, done after 1 st and 3 rd cycle. Interestingly, CdS nanorod clusters changed their shape to individual nanorods and CQD@CdS12NFs showed little distorted shape, while CQD@CdS24NFs remained same even after 30 h ( Fig S10 ). This shows the long-term stability of CQD@CdS24NFs photocatalyst, which corelates with its longer lifetime (Fig. 5 a) and stable photocurrent vs time (Fig. 5 d) results.

Although many literatures have been reported synthesis of carbon assisted CdS photocatalyst, but amount of H2 generation rate was significantly low that it hinders applicability in real world (Fig. 7 e and Table S6 ). Here, synthesis CdS nanostructures with different morphology and CQD was used as co-catalyst that helped to attain reduced photo corrosion of CdS with higher stability. The superior catalytic activity of nanoflowers can be attributed to following factors: (i) CdS with nanoflowers shape tends to show longer charge carrier lifetime with more reaction sites lead to increase in water-splitting action. (ii) Additionally, CdS having wurtzite phase with a (002) oriented facet, helps to attain better stability. (iv) The presence of Svacancy helps in increasing the efficacy of CdS photocatalysts.

To further understand the effect of Svacancy on the promotion of charge carrier separation of CdS nanoflowers, electronic structure calculations based on the DFT were performed as shown in Fig. 8
a and Fig S9 . The Fig S9 show the band structure of bare CdS and CQD loaded CdS with Svacancy having Eg of 0.7 eV and 0.5 eV respectively, which are smaller than experimental Eg (2.27 eV and 2.38 eV) due to well-known bandgap underestimation of standard DFT. The introduction of CQDs and Svacancy , introduced a new state below the conduction band minima and above the valence band maxima, leading to the reduction in the bandgap (Eg) of CdS. Moreover, presence of Svacancy enhances the affinity to attract electrons, causing photoactivated electrons to accumulate in Svacancy . As a result, trapped electrons significantly take part in H2O reduction and thereby serve as active sites for photocatalytic HER.

Based on the following observations a possible mechanism for photocatalytic HER has been shown in Fig. 8 c. When light falls on CdS electrons of VB migrate towards CB and leaves a hole behind at VB. In Bare CdS these generated holes have a strong tendency to oxidize CdS. To overcome this, we use TEA and Methanol as SR which reacts with holes and thereby avoids photo corrosion of CdS. Further, band structure optimization also becomes crucial to improve H2 generation efficacy. In order to obtain high H2 yield it is required to have negative CB potential to reduce the water molecule. As shown in Fig. 8 b Bare CdS NRs have a lower Eg of 2.27 eV and showed a decrease in H2 generation rate due to its positive CB potential. Whereas although CQD@CdS12NFs have a larger bandgap showed an increase in H2 generation rate due to its negative CB potential [75,76]. Thus, it becomes crucial to maintain balance between bandgap and band potential positions to ensure proper flow of electrons and leading to higher HER activity. Apart from this presence of Svacancy that acts as trap centers providing an effective way to separate the individual electron-hole pairs and allows them to take part in reaction instead of getting recombined [66]. This helps in increasing the H2 generation efficiency of CdS photocatalysts, proving a pivotal way for its industrial use to meet the energy demands.

In summary, size dependent CdS nanoflowers for visible light responsive hydrogen generation and Rhodamine B dye degradation were synthesized by solvothermal method by adjusting the reaction time and solvent mixture. It showed the CdS nanoflowers of larger size showed an enhancement in catalytic activity. However, they were not able to withstand photostability and CdS nanoflowers of smaller size showed a comparably low photocatalytic efficiency but highly stable up to 30 h under continuous illumination. This increased stability was due to the presence of Svacancy and exposed (002) crystal facts of CdS. Further, a significant enhancement in photocatalytic activity was found with the addition of Carbon Quantum Dots that act as strong electron acceptors helping in reducing the charge carrier recombination rate. We aspire that this research can furnish a reliable approach to devising cost-effective and high-performance hybrid photocatalysts, that can effectively support industrial applications.
